One caveat: this does not help IRAM directly. IRAM overflow is governed by the linker script and actual `.iram0.text` placement. The RAM size estimate is just PlatformIOâ€™s static RAM budget check for `.data/.bss`.

user_prompt: If the application uses PSRAM and is based on ESP32 rev. 3 (ECO3), setting CONFIG_ESP32_REV_MIN to 3 disables PSRAM bug workarounds, saving 10 KB or more of IRAM.

agent_reply: Your chip reports `Revision: 3.01`, so it is ESP32 ECO3-or-newer. The setting is meant exactly for this case: if you guarantee the firmware only runs on ESP32 rev 3+ silicon, ESP-IDF can drop the old PSRAM cache bug workarounds and save a meaningful chunk of IRAM.
- `Embedded PSRAM: No` is not the blocker. That only means PSRAM is not inside the ESP32 package. Core2-style boards can still have external SPI PSRAM.
- This is only safe if every device you ship/use is rev 3+. If an older ESP32 rev 0/1/2 board gets this firmware, expect boot refusal or bad behavior.
So you are currently paying for the old silicon workaround. Setting min rev to 3 should let IDF drop a bunch of the `CONFIG_SPIRAM_CACHE_LIB*_IN_IRAM` baggage.

My take: this is one of the better IRAM buybacks available to you. Iâ€™d try `CONFIG_ESP32_REV_MIN_3=y` / `CONFIG_ESP32_REV_MIN=3` before the more dangerous ISR-related FreeRTOS/ringbuf flash-placement options. It can run PlatformIOâ€™s project/config processing quietly so IntelliSense, environments, dependencies, and generated build metadata stay current.
In your project, that likely means:
- It may run PlatformIOâ€™s Arduino-ESP32/ESP-IDF integration scripts.
- Those scripts generate or regenerate SDK config files used by the build system.
- If `sdkconfig.defaults` is missing, the integration can recreate it from the defaults/custom config source.
- VS Code C/C++ metadata may also refresh include paths, defines, and compile commands.

So the important bit: `sdkconfig.defaults` is not being recreated because VS Code â€œlikes that file.â€ It is being recreated because PlatformIO/ESP-IDF treats it as part of the build configuration pipeline.

user_prompt: I am looking at my serial terminal, I see "NVS initialized" as the last message, I have Verbose logging configured, why am I not seeing more messages? did something shutdown the serial port?
agent_reply: No, I donâ€™t see anything shutting down the serial port.
Whatâ€™s happening is likely logging-level mismatch:
- `NVS initialized` is a raw `Serial.printf()` in [all_init.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:100), so it appears.
- The very next normal boot message is `ESP_LOGI(...)` in [all_init.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/all_init.cpp:326), not `Serial.printf`.

- Your app defines verbose flags in `platformio.ini`, but the prebuilt ESP-IDF sdkconfig is still capped at `CONFIG_LOG_DEFAULT_LEVEL_ERROR` and `CONFIG_LOG_MAXIMUM_LEVEL=1`. That means `ESP_LOGI`, `ESP_LOGD`, etc. wonâ€™t show. Error logs should still show.
I also checked M5Unified: it only calls `Serial.begin()` if `cfg.serial_baudrate` is nonzero, and our `init_m5()` does not set that. No `Serial.end()` in our code.

So the port is probably alive; normal ESP-IDF info/debug logs are just being suppressed. Best quick diagnostic is to add a few temporary `Serial.printf()` breadcrumbs after `check_reset_flag()`, `init_m5()`, `init_gui()`, and `BattTracker::shutdownIfNeeded()` to see where boot is actually reaching.

agent_reply: That crash is not the serial port. Itâ€™s an interrupt watchdog in the Bluedroid HFP client on core 0 during service-level connection. Core 1 was in `AudioFileRecorder::pump()` trying to take the recorder mutex, even though there may be no active recording.
I made the recorder pump path much less intrusive:
- Switched the recorder active flag to an atomic fast check in [AudioFileRecorder.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/AudioFileRecorder/AudioFileRecorder.cpp:66).
- `pump()` now immediately returns when no recording is active.
- [main.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/src/main.cpp:345) now skips calling `pump()` entirely unless `needsPump()` is true.
This should stop the idle main loop from hammering FreeRTOS mutex/queue internals during Bluetooth pairing/HFP setup.
Verified with `pio run -e thefly_sec0`: build succeeded. If the watchdog still fires on pairing, the next suspect is the temporary HFP debug build flags in `platformio.ini`.

agent_reply: That trace is still the same Bluedroid HFP SLC path, now specifically dying while sending `AT+BAC` for codec negotiation.

I added a targeted workaround: [BluetoothManager.cpp](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/lib/BluetoothManager/BluetoothManager.cpp) now wraps Bluedroidâ€™s internal `bta_hf_client_send_at_bac()` and skips straight to the next SLC step (`AT+CIND`). [platformio.ini](D:/GithubRepos/The-Fly/platformio.ini) adds the linker wrap flag. Your crash is coming from the prebuilt Arduino/IDF mDNS archive:
So the real fix would be to rebuild or replace that library/package with mDNS compiled for PSRAM allocation, roughly:
or the equivalent sdkconfig change before rebuilding the framework libs.

I’d treat this as a platform package rebuild, not just a project config tweak. A clean path would be:

2. Rebuild the Arduino ESP32 precompiled libs/package.
3. Point `platformio.ini` / `platform_packages` at your rebuilt package so the build is reproducible.
I would not jump straight to moving the mDNS task stack to PSRAM unless needed. The failure is packet/bookkeeping allocation, and using SPIRAM for those allocations is the narrower change.

That should tell us whether mDNS is failing because internal heap is low/fragmented while PSRAM still looks huge.
